narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and academic record were carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed key aspects of your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 25, family income in the middle bracket, and your rankings in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, along with your plan to attend full-time. It ultimately predicted a 96% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants most likely to succeed and become practicing attorneys. The three most influential factors in this decision were your third-year school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your first-year school decile ranking, all of which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure rather than success. Your third-year decile of 1—far below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model a significant drop or stagnation in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your LSAT score of 25, much lower than the average of about 37, reinforced concerns about your readiness for the rigors of legal exams. Your first-year decile of 1, also well below the average, added to this by highlighting early academic challenges that didn't improve over time. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who tend to rank higher in their classes and score much stronger on the LSAT, your profile stood out as higher risk in these core areas. While factors like your full-time status (common among 93% of students) and middle-income background were neutral or less influential, they couldn't offset the weight of your academic rankings and test score. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar Hispanic communities overcome odds with targeted preparation—but the model focuses on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your class rankings through additional coursework or community college refreshers, and exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for first-generation or underrepresented students like yourself. With focused improvements in these areas, you could reapply stronger next cycle and show the model you're on track for success.
